questions.question: What does the term 'free-running' refer to in the context of biological rhythms?

answerOptions.text: The rhythm of an animal that is deprived of external cues about the time of day.
answerOptions.rationale: In the absence of external synchronisers, an endogenous clock maintains its own natural period, which often slightly deviates from 24 hours.

answerOptions.text: The rapid phase shift experienced when travelling across several time zones.
answerOptions.rationale: This describes jet lag or a phase shift, which is an adjustment to new external cues rather than the absence of them.

answerOptions.text: A sleep cycle that lacks any periods of rapid-eye-movement (REM) sleep.
answerOptions.rationale: Free-running relates to the timing and periodicity of the sleep-wake cycle as a whole, not the internal structure of sleep stages.

answerOptions.text: The process of using a 'zeitgeber' to synchronise internal activity with the environment.
answerOptions.rationale: This process is known as entrainment, which is the opposite of a free-running state.

questions.hint: Think about how a clock behaves when it is not being reset by an external time-giver.

questions.question: In the hypothalamic molecular clock, which proteins form a dimer to promote the transcription of the $per$ and $cry$ genes?

answerOptions.text: Clock and Cycle (Bmal1)
answerOptions.rationale: These two proteins bind together as a dimer to enhance the transcription of the Period and Cryptochrome genes.

answerOptions.text: Per and Cry
answerOptions.rationale: While these proteins do dimerise, their role is to inhibit the activity of the Clock/Cycle dimer, not to promote transcription.

answerOptions.text: Melanopsin and Glutamate
answerOptions.rationale: Melanopsin is a photopigment and glutamate is a neurotransmitter involved in entrainment, not the core transcriptional feedback loop.

answerOptions.text: Alpha and Beta
answerOptions.rationale: These terms refer to EEG rhythms seen during different states of arousal, rather than molecular clock proteins.

questions.hint: Identify the pair of molecules that start the cycle by acting on the cell's DNA.

questions.question: What was the primary finding of the SCN transplant experiments involving the $\tau$ mutation in hamsters?

answerOptions.text: The endogenous period of the recipient hamster changed to match the period of the donor SCN.
answerOptions.rationale: This demonstrated that the SCN itself is the source of the endogenous rhythm, as the host adopted the 20-hour or 24-hour cycle of the donor tissue.

answerOptions.text: The recipient hamsters became permanently arrhythmic regardless of the donor's genotype.
answerOptions.rationale: Transplants actually restored rhythmicity to lesioned animals, provided the donor tissue was healthy.

answerOptions.text: The SCN required direct synaptic connections to the hypothalamus to drive circadian rhythms.
answerOptions.rationale: Encapsulation experiments showed that chemical signals from the SCN could drive rhythms even without synaptic connections.

answerOptions.text: Light information was required for the transplanted SCN to establish any rhythm at all.
answerOptions.rationale: The experiments were conducted in constant conditions to reveal the endogenous rhythm, showing the clock works without light cues.

questions.hint: Focus on whose internal timing—the host's or the donor's—prevailed after the procedure.

questions.question: Which photopigment is specifically found in the retinal ganglion cells that project to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) in mammals?

answerOptions.text: Melanopsin
answerOptions.rationale: This photopigment allows certain ganglion cells to be intrinsically photosensitive, carrying light information directly to the SCN.

answerOptions.text: Rhodopsin
answerOptions.rationale: Rhodopsin is the photopigment found in rods, which are primarily involved in low-light vision rather than circadian entrainment.

answerOptions.text: Cryptochrome
answerOptions.rationale: In mammals, Cryptochrome is a protein involved in the internal molecular clock feedback loop, not a retinal photopigment.

answerOptions.text: Cone opsin
answerOptions.rationale: Opsins in cones provide colour vision and form vision, but the circadian system can function even in their absence.

questions.hint: Consider the pigment most sensitive to blue light frequencies that informs the biological clock.

questions.question: During which stage of sleep are 'sleep spindles' and 'K-complexes' the defining electroencephalographic (EEG) features?

answerOptions.text: Stage 2 sleep (NREM 2)
answerOptions.rationale: Stage 2 is characterised by periodic bursts of 12-14 Hz waves (spindles) and sharp negative potentials (K-complexes).

answerOptions.text: Stage 1 sleep (NREM 1)
answerOptions.rationale: Stage 1 is characterised by vertex spikes and a slowing of the heart rate, but does not yet show spindles.

answerOptions.text: Stage 3 sleep (SWS)
answerOptions.rationale: Stage 3 is defined by large-amplitude, very slow $\Delta$ waves representing widespread cortical synchronisation.

answerOptions.text: REM sleep
answerOptions.rationale: REM sleep features a desynchronised EEG similar to wakefulness, along with complete muscle atonia.

questions.hint: This stage occurs after light drowsiness but before the appearance of deep, slow delta waves.

questions.question: How does the distribution of sleep stages typically change across a single night in a healthy young adult?

answerOptions.text: Stage 3 sleep is more prominent early in the night, while REM episodes lengthen towards the morning.
answerOptions.rationale: The first half of the night is dominated by slow-wave sleep, whereas the second half contains more REM sleep.

answerOptions.text: REM sleep occurs only in the first 90 minutes, followed by continuous Stage 3 sleep.
answerOptions.rationale: Sleep consists of repeating cycles; REM usually occurs at the end of each 90-110 minute cycle throughout the night.

answerOptions.text: Stage 2 sleep gradually disappears as the night progresses, being replaced by Stage 1.
answerOptions.rationale: Stage 2 remains a significant portion of total sleep time (about 50%) throughout the night's cycles.

answerOptions.text: The duration of sleep cycles increases from 90 minutes at the start to 180 minutes by dawn.
answerOptions.rationale: While the composition of stages within the cycles changes, the cycle length itself remains relatively consistent.

questions.hint: Think about why you are more likely to remember vivid dreams if you wake up late in the morning.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic change in sleep patterns associated with healthy ageing?

answerOptions.text: A significant reduction in the total amount of Stage 3 (SWS) sleep.
answerOptions.rationale: By age 60, humans spend about half as much time in Stage 3 as they did at age 20, and it may disappear entirely by age 90.

answerOptions.text: An increase in the total percentage of REM sleep to nearly 50%.
answerOptions.rationale: High percentages of REM sleep (up to 50%) are characteristic of infancy, not old age.

answerOptions.text: The consolidation of sleep into a single, uninterrupted 8-hour block.
answerOptions.rationale: Ageing is typically associated with more frequent awakenings and fragmented sleep patterns.

answerOptions.text: A shift in circadian rhythm that makes elderly individuals 'night owls'.
answerOptions.rationale: The shift towards being a 'late riser' is most prominent at puberty; elderly people often tend to wake earlier.

questions.hint: Consider which stage of deep sleep, linked to growth hormone and cognitive function, declines most sharply.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the 'glymphatic system' during sleep?
questions.questionLaTeX: What is the primary function of the 'glymphatic system' during sleep?

answerOptions.text: The disposal of metabolic toxins and waste products, such as $\beta$-amyloid, from the brain.
answerOptions.rationale: Glia control a network of channels that flush out waste products much faster during sleep than during wakefulness.

answerOptions.text: The consolidation of declarative memories into long-term storage.
answerOptions.rationale: While sleep helps memory, this is generally attributed to neural activity patterns rather than the glymphatic drainage system.

answerOptions.text: The regulation of body temperature to conserve energy during the night.
answerOptions.rationale: Thermoregulation is a separate physiological process, and in REM sleep, it is actually impaired.

answerOptions.text: The synthesis of new proteins used to repair muscle tissue throughout the body.
answerOptions.rationale: The glymphatic system is specific to the brain's waste clearance, not systemic protein synthesis.

questions.hint: Think of this as the brain's unique plumbing or 'waste management' system.

questions.question: Which biological rhythm is characterised by a period that is shorter than a day, such as the 90-minute cycle of sleep stages?

answerOptions.text: Ultradian
answerOptions.rationale: Ultradian rhythms have a frequency greater than once per day, with periods ranging from minutes to several hours.

answerOptions.text: Infradian
answerOptions.rationale: Infradian rhythms have periods longer than a day, such as the 28-day human menstrual cycle.

answerOptions.text: Circannual
answerOptions.rationale: Circannual rhythms occur on a roughly annual basis, often regulating seasonal behaviours like breeding.

answerOptions.text: Diurnal
answerOptions.rationale: Diurnal refers to being active during the light periods of a 24-hour circadian cycle, not a shorter period.

questions.hint: The prefix of this term comes from the Latin for 'beyond', indicating a higher frequency than daily.

questions.question: What unique adaptation allows dolphins and certain migratory birds to remain vigilant or active while sleeping?

answerOptions.text: Unilateral sleep, where one hemisphere sleeps while the other remains awake.
answerOptions.rationale: This allows dolphins to surface for air and birds to watch for predators or continue flying over long distances.

answerOptions.text: The complete elimination of NREM sleep in favour of brief REM bursts.
answerOptions.rationale: Dolphins actually appear to lack REM sleep because its associated muscle atonia is incompatible with surfacing to breathe.

answerOptions.text: An endogenous clock that runs on a 12-hour rather than 24-hour cycle.
answerOptions.rationale: These animals still maintain circadian rhythms; the adaptation relates to how the brain engages in sleep stages.

answerOptions.text: The use of the pineal gland as a 'third eye' to detect predators during deep SWS.
answerOptions.rationale: While some non-mammalian vertebrates have light-sensitive pineal glands, it does not allow for 'waking' behaviour during sleep.

questions.hint: Consider how the brain might divide the 'labour' of sleeping and staying alert.

questions.question: Fatal familial insomnia is associated with the degeneration of which brain regions?

answerOptions.text: Cortex and thalamus
answerOptions.rationale: Autopsies of patients with this inherited prion disease show significant damage to these areas, which likely causes the inability to sleep.

answerOptions.text: Suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) and optic chiasm
answerOptions.rationale: While the SCN is the master clock, its specific degeneration is not the hallmark of this fatal insomnia disorder.

answerOptions.text: Retina and pineal gland
answerOptions.rationale: These areas are involved in light detection and melatonin release, but they are not the primary sites of cortical degeneration in this disease.

answerOptions.text: Hippocampus and amygdala
answerOptions.rationale: These regions are more typically associated with memory and emotion rather than the fatal sleep loss seen in this condition.

questions.hint: The disease results in large holes in the frontal areas and affects the brain's sensory relay station.

questions.question: Why is REM sleep often referred to as 'paradoxical sleep'?

answerOptions.text: The EEG appears like an active, waking brain, yet the skeletal muscles are completely flaccid.
answerOptions.rationale: This contradiction between intense brain activity and profound muscle relaxation defines the 'paradox'.

answerOptions.text: It is the only sleep stage where dreaming is impossible.
answerOptions.rationale: On the contrary, REM sleep is the stage most closely associated with vivid, cinematic dreaming.

answerOptions.text: The body's temperature rises significantly while the heart rate drops to its lowest point.
answerOptions.rationale: In REM, both heart rate and respiration become irregular and variable, and thermoregulation is actually impaired.

answerOptions.text: It occurs only in the absence of an endogenous circadian clock.
answerOptions.rationale: REM sleep is a regular part of the sleep cycle driven and organised by circadian and ultradian rhythms.

questions.hint: Think about the contradiction between what the brainwaves show and what the body's muscles are doing.
